coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene and graphene quantum dots: A review onsynthesis and applications in energy, sensors and environment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Advances in Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, Manpreet
Kaur, Manmeet
Sharma, Virender K.
coredata.dc:description: Doping of nitrogen is a promising strategy to modulate chemical, electronic, and structural functionalities of graphene (G)and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for their outstanding properties in energy and environmental applications.This paper reviews various synthesis approaches of nitrogen-doped graphene (N-G) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs).;Thermal, ultrasonic, solvothermal, hydrothermal, and electron-beam methods have been applied to synthesize N-G and N-GQDs.These nitrogen-doped carbon materials are characterized to obtain their structural configurations in order to achieve better performance in their applications compared to only either graphene or graphene quantum dots.Both N-G and N-GQDs may be converted into functional materials by integrating with other compounds such as metal oxides/nitrides, polymers, and semiconductors.These functional materials demonstrate superior performance over N-G and N-GQDs materials.Examples of applications of N-G and N-GQDs include supercapacitors, batteries, sensors, fuel cells, solar cells, and photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene and graphene quantum dots: A review onsynthesis and applications in energy, sensors and environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doping of nitrogen is a promising strategy to modulate chemical, electronic, and structural functionalities of graphene (G)and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for their outstanding properties in energy and environmental applications.This paper reviews various synthesis approaches of nitrogen-doped graphene (N-G) and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs).;Thermal, ultrasonic, solvothermal, hydrothermal, and electron-beam methods have been applied to synthesize N-G and N-GQDs.These nitrogen-doped carbon materials are characterized to obtain their structural configurations in order to achieve better performance in their applications compared to only either graphene or graphene quantum dots.Both N-G and N-GQDs may be converted into functional materials by integrating with other compounds such as metal oxides/nitrides, polymers, and semiconductors.These functional materials demonstrate superior performance over N-G and N-GQDs materials.Examples of applications of N-G and N-GQDs include supercapacitors, batteries, sensors, fuel cells, solar cells, and photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the most important issues of the 21st century is to secure sustainability of energy that meets the global energy demand.Consumption of energy keeps increasing, for example 30% increase in the period of 1987 to 2012 [1]. Technological solution to meet the energy need of the society must not only be clean and efficient but also affordable [1,2]. Among the various catalysts applied to produce and transport energy, carbon-based nanomaterials (i.e.,metal free catalysts) have received profound attention in advanced oxidation processes to remove organic pollutants [3–7]. These nanomaterials have shown remarkable performance in reactions relevance to energy generation and storage such as hydration of allynes, fuel cells, oxidative dehydrogenation, and oxidation of numerous alcohols [8–10].

Another emerging issue of the century is to provide clean water to human population.According to a report of the United Nations and World Health Organization (WHO), more than 2 billion people lack safe drinking water at home [11]. More than 360,000 children die every year under 5 years of age due to water related diseases.With an increase in pollution of drinking water resources (e.g.,groundwater and surface water), meeting the demand of purified water to population is a challenge of great magnitude [12, 13].Water has also strong effect on energy production, thus affecting the economy of both developed and developing countries.Carbon-based nanomaterials may play important role in this century because of their potential applications in energy production and water remediation [14].

Carbon has different forms that contain changeable micro-textures (or various morphologies).Carbon has many allotropic forms, which include diamond, black carbon, carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, and graphene [15, 16].Among these different forms, graphene is one of the most researched nanomaterials in the last few years due to its various energy and environmental applications [17–20].

Graphene is a flat monolayer of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice with a distance of 0.142 nm between two carbon atoms [21]. It is a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice that forms the building blocks of some important allotropes. Graphene can be converted into three-dimensional (3-D) graphite by stacking or to one dimensional (1-D) nanotubes by rolling [22]. Graphene can also transform to zero-dimensional (0-D) fullerenes through wrapping. Graphene involves the π-conjugation, which results in its extraordinary electrical, mechanical and thermal properties [23]. Graphene is found to be impermeable to liquids and gases [24]. In comparison to the most effective materials, graphene has higher current density and higher thermal conductivity. Graphene possesses specific surface area of 2630 m2/g, which is similar to the activated carbon but much higher than specific surface area of the carbon black (smaller than 900 m2/g) or carbon nanotubes (from 100 to 1000m2/g). Because of its extraordinary properties, applications of graphene are in nano-systems and nanodevices [25].Graphene is the only form of carbon in which atoms are available from both sides for carrying out chemical reactions [26].

Graphene oxide (GO), a functionalized form of graphene, is obtained by exfoliating graphite. Graphene oxide has attracted renewal interests due to its superior properties like mechanical stability, large surface area, and tunable electrical and optical properties [27–29]. GO is a good candidate for coordinating to other molecules or materials because of important functional groups present on its surface i.e. hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxy groups. Due to its great structural diversity, GO and its composites can be used for energy storage and environmental protection including photocatalyst for water splitting, water purification, and removal of air pollutants [30–33].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a few atoms thick graphene and their size is less than 30 nm. GQDs possess excellent features such as large diameter, high surface area, and good surface grafting using conjugated π-π network and other physical properties [34]. These properties include highly tunable photoluminescence, remarkable UV-blocking property, good biocompatibility, unique spin property, and high photo stability. It possesses a wide range of applications in ion detection, bio-imaging, photo catalysts, photo detectors, electrochemical luminescence, optical sensors and photovoltaic devices [35–39].Doping of graphene and GQDs with heteroatoms has led to improve their catalytic activities towards oxidation-reduction reactions which are a prerequisite for energy storage/conversion.

Introduction of heteroatom (e.g.,boron, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorous, and sulfur) into carbon lattice can adequately disorientate the electron network, which is homogeneously conjugated.This network regulates the surface properties by adjusting the charge distribution and spinning culture of doped domains [40]. Nitrogen is adjacent to carbon in periodic table thus possess similarity in atomic radius (0.70 Å) with carbon (0.77 Å) and greater electronegativity (χN = 3.04) than C (χc = 2.55), makes it easier to incorporate nitrogen into carbon network via substitution doping. For example, metallic N-doped graphene sheets[41].Electron states of graphene can be intrinsically altered by N-doping and subsequently it endows pristine graphene with improved activity towards specific reactions and catalysis [42–44].

In the next two sections, strategies used in the synthesis of N-G and N-GQD are presented.In these sections, physical and chemical properties of N-G and N-GQDs and their applications related to energy storage and environmental remediation processes are also discussed.

Nitrogen can be introduced into graphene lattice using bottom-up synthesis approach [48].Various nitrogen-containing substances like ammonia, ammonium salts and organic compounds have been used as precursors for nitrogen[49–51].A summary of different methods used in preparing N-G is discussed in this section.

Thermal annealing is a heat treatment, which alters the physical and chemical properties of a material (e.g.,enhancement inductility and decrease in hardness). This method is also used for making composite materials. This method involves heating of material above its recrystallization temperature, maintaining an appropriate temperature, followed by cooling [52]. NiFe@N-doped graphenemicrotube (NiFe-NGMT) compositeswere synthesized by thermal annealing of nickel nitroprusside (NiFe(CN)5NO.2H2O) under inert condition of N2 gas [53].These composites consisted of Ni-Fe alloy particles enveloped in N-Gmicrotubes with a diameter of 1–2 μm. The diameter of tubular graphene was tuned to 50–200 nm by adjusting pyrolysis temperature. Electronic and chemical properties of graphene have also been modified by carrying out N-doping via thermal annealing of graphene with urea as a nitrogen source and also by varying the annealing temperature and time [54]. Thermal annealing of graphene lowers interlayer distance,which was ascribed to the removal of functional groups and other impurities.An annealing at lower temperature results in formation of monolayer graphene films.Comparatively, high temperature thermal annealing results in multilayered graphene films [55]. Additionally, high temperature thermal annealing results in greater removal of functional groups, leading to the formation of multilayer graphene films.

In the ultrasonication method, sound energy is applied to agitate the particles in the solution, which causes physical and chemical transformations. A nanocomposite consisting of chitosan and N-G grown on Hierarchial porous carbon (N-PG-G/CS) was prepared by the ultrasonication method. Polymeric triblock surfactant, polystyrene microspheres, and resol were used as soft template, hard template and carbon precursors, respectively [56]. The synthesized hybrid contained carbon micro blocks interconnected with graphene. It was found that like porous carbon or graphene, Hierarchial porous carbon-graphene (N-PC-G) was hydrophobic and had agglomeration tendency.Both of these properties limit applications of the N-PC-G.In this method, the mixture of NH4OH and GO was ultrasonicated, followed by vacuum filtration. Thermal treatment of GO-NH3 cake resulted in the formation of N-G.

Pyridinic-rich nitrogen doped nanoplates (PRGOs) were synthesized using an acid-catalyzed dehydration reaction between GO and 3,4-diaminopyridine [57]. Pyridinic nitrogen could be directly introduced into graphitic network through newly formed imine and pyrazine linkages. The structure of PRGOs was confirmed by spectroscopic and microscopic analyses. Nitrogen content in PRGOs was controlled over a wide range (ie.,1.9–9.1 wt%) by a simple variation in feed ratios of reactants.

In another synthesis method of N-G, a wet-reaction between monoketone, present in graphene oxide (GO), and monoamine produced imine functionalized GO.The reaction between α-diketone present in graphene oxide and 1,2-diamine yielded pyrazine ring functionalizedGO.The heat treatment of these synthesized GOs yielded high quality N-G nanoplatelets [58].

Solvothermal method combines the benefits of both sol-gel and hydrothermal routes. This solvation method can be used to control the particle size of synthesized material. For example, Fe3O4 nanocrystals anchored on N-G nanosheets had controlled size, shape distribution, and crystallinity [59]. Electron microscopy measurements showed that the Fe3O4nanocrystals were uniformly dispersed on N-G nanosheets. In the structure, Fe3O4nanocrystals were chemically bonded on the nitrogen doped graphene surface.The solvation method was also used to prepare fluorine (F)and nitrogen (N)co-doped graphene paper (FNGP) electrode [60]. Interestingly, co-doping of F and N in graphene network could enhance the structural defects and also enlarged the graphene interlayer distance [61].

Hydrothermal method involves dissolving minerals in hot water under high pressure, leading to the formation of single crystals [62].This method can be used for synthesizing materials having high vapour pressure near their melting points. Magnetic 3D N-G gels were obtained via one-pot hydrothermal synthesis. Doping of nitrogen resulted in reduction of graphene oxide and generation of Fe3O4 particles, which was accompanied by 3-Dself-assembly [63]. The synthesized magnetic N-G gels were considered to be inter-linked as the porous networks of graphene sheets.

Fig. 3 represents an example of the preparation of nitrogen-doped three dimensional reduced graphene oxide (N-3DrGO) catalyst using graphene oxide, melamine and formaldehyde [64]. N-contents in the N-3DrGO material varied from 3.12 to 9.69%, which could be controlled by adjusting feed mass ratios of GO and melamine. This synthesized N-G had a typical thin layered structure with high specific surface area.

Chemical vapor deposition is a process usually used for making high performance and high quality solid materials. For example, an application of this process in semiconductor industry to produce films [65]. Microwave plasma torch coupled with plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition method was used for manipulating the thickness as well as defect density of few layer graphene sheets at temperature upto 300 ° C [66]. This was done under argon atmosphere to control graphene defects and thickness of 2–10 layers during a graphene growth step. Nitrogen content of the obtained material varied from 4.7–13.3. N-doping was tuned by controlling graphene defect density with intensity.This synthesis approach could produce different functionalities of the resultant materials.

Various approaches discussed in this section highlight the facile synthesis of N-G by thermal annealing, ultrasonication, hydrothermal, solvothermal and plasma enhanced chemical vapor deposition methods. Further work on combination of different synthesis approaches may optimize the quality and quantity of N-G. Elucidation of mechanism involved in synthesis studies on the yield of N-G is needed to improve properties of N-G for various applications.

A difference between electro negativity of nitrogen and carbon in the N-G resulted in numerous applications in electronics [67, 68], energy storage devices [69–71] and catalysis [72, 73].These applications are broadly divided into two categories viz. optical/catalytic properties. Conductive properties have been explored for their applications in batteries and supercapacitors.Optical and catalytic properties are also envisioned to have their applications. The applications are summarized in Table 1 .Below are examples of improved conductive, optical and electrocatalytic properties, possessed by N-G.

Conductive properties are important parameters for improving the performance of batteries and supercapacitors. They are important alternate for developing highly efficient and eco-friendly electrochemical energy sources [74, 75].

Various metals, metal oxides, polymers and their composites have been recognized as good charge storage materials. Graphene and its derivatives are the potential candidates for supercapacitors due to their low cost, durability, and multi-functionality. Possibility of graphene as a supercapacitor can be further increased by means of doping with nitrogen [76–78]. Examples are presented in Fig. 4 in which storage capacity of 3D graphene increased by N-doping.A wettability at the surface was enhanced by electrolytes. Supercapacitor performance of RGO films is shown in Fig. 4a, which demonstrate high specific capacitance owing to large surface area and high electrical conductivity [77]. Significantly, doping with nitrogen induced different kinds of functionalities on the surface to hold pseudocapacitive materials. Redox reactions among different oxidation states assisted in charge storage by these materials.For example, redox reactions of polyaniline, a capacitive polymer, led to the formation of N-doped carbon nanotube-polyaniline composite, which had amplification of charge storage capacity due to synergistic effect (Fig. 4b). The formation of ternary composites further increased the capacity by deposition of transition metal oxides and polyaniline at N-doped carbon nanotubes (Fig. 4c). Increased activity in case of N-graphene as compared to simple graphene was due to effective modulation and activation of graphene surface because of N-doping. Moreover, Natoms with excessive valence electrons provide additional π-electrons in graphitic network. Also, electronegativity difference between N and C offers a reduced work function, higher surface energy, and tunable polarization of graphitic plane, which are not possible in the case of simple graphene.Basically, these properties increased the performance of N-G in comparison with graphene. Nitrogen doping is obtained using different precursors, yielding pyrollic N, pyridinic N, and quaternary N.Significantly, each type of nitrogen doping enhances different properties. Synthesis of N-doped graphene by CVD method displayed doubling of the reversible discharge capacity of N-graphene relative to pristine graphene due to elevated Li-ion concentration [79].

N-doped porous reduced graphene (N-pGr) has been used for fabricating solid state supercapacitor devices. Porous nature of N-pGrincreased electromotive surface area of electrical double layer formation that facilitated the faster movement of ions of electrolytes inside the electrode. N-doping changed the electrical properties of the electrode material and improved the overall capacitance of fabricated device. This good capacitance retention allowed strong flexible solid-state supercapacitor with N-pGr material [80]. Pyridinic-richN-G nanoplates (PRGOs) showed high performance of supercapacitors including enhanced long-term stability and specific capacitance [57].

Two-dimensional sheets of active materials of energy storage devices have limited applications due to surface area restrictions.This was circumvented by developing three-dimensional structures having high specific surface area. The 3D graphene-nanoparticle aerogels were tested as supercapacitors using typical nitrogen doped graphene/Fe3O4 structures, synthesized by a hydrothermal method [81].The NG/Fe3O4 supercapacitor showed enhancement in specific capacitance upto 153%, which was retained even after 1000cycles.

N-G, supported by water soluble Prussian blue (PB) NPs, was fabricated as a hybrid battery-supercapacitor electrode [82].This electrode exhibited an excellent performance with highest capacity of 660 Cg−1 at 1 Ag−1, which was higher than that of pure PB NPs and N-graphene electrodes. Synergistic effect of PB NPs and N-graphene prevented N-graphene from swelling and shrinking, which caused increase in the cyclic stability to 84.7% in comparison with pure N-G.

Manipulation of basal plane structure of graphene was also reported for designing advanced energy conversion materials [83]. Size of defects could be extended from atomic scale to the nano scale in the graphene basal plane.This allowed synthesizing graphene with in plane holes by multi-steps oxidation and reduction of defective graphene oxide at low temperature. Holey GO was synthesized by using abundantly available defective graphite mineral. A reduction of holey GO led to the formation of N- holey graphene, which had high capacitance of 439 F/cm3. This may be an attractive material for storing energy due to low cost and high specific capacitance. This inexpensive byproduct could be augmented for producing first generation bio-fuel [84].

Graphene incorporated N-doped activated carbons (GNACs) were studied as electrode materials of supercapacitors [85]. At the activation temperature of 700 ° C, the resultant sample exhibited hierarchically porous texture with high specific surface area and efficient ions diffusion channels. Significantly, nitrogen, oxygen-functionalizedsurface with apparent pseudocapacitance contribution and high wettability could deliver a moderate capacitance that had high rate capability and good cycleability when used as a supercapacitor electrode. High catalytic activity of the redox reaction of pyrocatechol/o-quinone pair in H2SO4 electrolyte enabled high pseudocapacitance [86].Under optimal pyrocatechol concentration in H2SO4 electrolyte, the electrode capacitance of GNAC increased by 4 folds; giving value of 512 F g−1 at 1 A g−1.This electrode had an excellent cycleability. Pyridinic-N was suggested to be responsible of such high catalytic activity.

N-G has been evaluated as an electrode material for batteries.For example, N-G has great utility in the field of lithium ion batteries. The modified electronic configuration may allow absorption of lithium ionsto increase the diffusion of ions through structural defects. These structural defects may further provide more binding sites for Li-ion coordination. N-G has exhibited more discharge capacity than pristine graphene because of dense surface defects [87]. However, N-doped materials possess limited intrinsic capacity in comparison with other anode materials such as Si [88], Sn, SiOx [89], SnOx [90] and FeOx [91].N-G with 3.13% nitrogen content, synthesized by heat treatment method [92], showed reversible capacities of 1043 mAh/g and 872 mAh/g in first and thirty-first cycles, respectively, at the current rate of 50mAh/g (Fig. 5a and 5b).However, the capacities reached up to 199 mAh/g at a current rate of 25 A/g (Fig. 5c and 5d).

Tin (Sn)-based materials have high capacity anodes in rechargeable Li-ion batteries. Sn NPs embedded in porous N-G like carbon network (Sn@PNGC) provided active sites for inserting Li ions and obtaining appreciable electron transport kinetics [93]. Sn@PNGC anode had improved stability, rate capability, and capacity.At 100 mA g−1 current, this anode gave a discharge capacity of 1129 mAh g−1 and reversible capacity of 595 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles. After 300cycles at 500 mA g−1,the rate capacity increased to 437 mAh g−1.

Graphene co-doped with N and S and supported on ZnFe2O4nanosphere showed discharge capacity of 2478.6 mAhg−1 at a rate of 300mAg−1. Reversible capacity and coulombic efficiency were 729 mAhg−1 and 99.36%, respectively, after 100 cycles. The composite showed better stability and rate capability for its use as an anodic material in Li ion batteries [94].

Nitrogen doped graphene nanosheets/sulfur (NGNSs/S) composite [95] and N-G immobilized lithium polysulfides [59] have been used as electrode materials. NGNSs/S composite provided an initial discharge capacity of 856.7 mAhg−1and a reversible capacity of 319.3 mAhg−1 at 0.1 C with a good recoverable rate capability.N-G based sulfur electrode gave capacity of 1200 mAh g−1 at 0.3 A g−1.This electrode exhibited good capacity retention with only 0.05% decayed in capacity per cycle after 300cycles at 0.75 A g−1.

Graphene paper co-doped with fluorine and nitrogen (FNGP) were used as an anode of sodium ion batteries [60]. Enhanced structural defects, enlarged graphene interlayer distance and presence of active sites improved Na ion storage capabilities. At 120 ° C, electrical conductivity increased due to presence of C-F bonds in an anode that allowed movement of electron in the FNGP.This anode exhibited reversible capacity of 56.3 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1 after 5000 cycles and had long cycling stability. The good tensile strength of 43.5±1.8 MPa along with promising electrochemical behavior of FNGP indicated its potential application as an anode in wearable, flexible, and rolled-up devices.

An interest in photocatalytical processes is motivated by simulating natural photosynthetic process to generate fuel such as hydrogen [96]. Pure graphene has zero band gap. N-doping of graphene improved the optical properties when present in the composite form. Also, N-Gr composites displayed red shift in the absorption edge [97]. Similar to that of reduced GO, the formation of N-Ti-O bonds may also lead to the formation of a localized state within the bandgap of TiO2 and results in narrow bandgap [98]. The presence of carbon in graphene derivatives decreased the reflection of light that enhanced the visible light absorption.

One more valence electron in nitrogen than that of carbon resulted in the formation of n-type semiconductor.This property could be used for the formation of composite with favorable p–n junction. A composite was synthesized that comprised 5–20 nm p-type MoS2 nanoplates, which were distributed over an n-typenitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [99]. This composite had multiple nanoscale p–n junctions. In the photocatalyst system, MoS2 acted both a catalyst and a photoactive component.In the composite, r-GO inhibited recombination of electron–hole. Basically, doping of r-GO with nitrogen turned graphene intop-MoS2/n-rGOhetero-structure, an n-type photoactive material, which generated higher photocurrent than that of the composite. This photocatalyst had the ability of generating hydrogen under ultraviolet to infrared wavelength range irradiation.

The photocatalytic activity of N-G was investigated by coupling it with CdS [100]. NGR/CdS composite showed twice the photocatalytic performance as that of reduced GO/CdS. In this photocatalyst, each graphitic N contributed 0.5 electrons to graphitic π network [101]. Resistance of N-Gwas found to be 100 times greater than graphene because of its unavoidable defects.This shortcoming was compensated by excessive π electrons from substituted nitrogen atom. This was supported by an experiment which showed that the thermally annealed N-Ghad increased conductivity of 40 Scm−1while the conductivity of reduced GO was only 3.5 Scm−1 [102]. This increase in density of N-G improved its electrical conductivity and also enhanced migration of electron-hole pairs to the surface of composite.This phenomena inhibited electron-hole recombination and hence resulted in enhanced photocatalytical activity [103]. A comparison of the photocurrent density of TiO2, RGO/TiO2 and N-GrTiO2 composites has been carried out. N-Gr composite displayed appreciable photocurrent density as compared to RGO composite and TiO2(Fig. 6a) [104]. The Nyquist plot of electrochemical impedance spectra revealed that the N-Gr composite exhibited small curvature of impedance relative to RGO(Fig. 6b).

Superconductors are of great interest in complementing Li ion batteries. 3D porous N-G hydrogel (N-RGOH) was examined as supercapacitor electrode in an ionic liquid electrolyte [105]. N-RGOH displayed porous structure and specific surface area was335.6 m2 g−1.N-GrOH- based supercapacitor device had high energy density of 94.5 WhKg−1. N-RGOH showed cyclic performances as a supercapacitor, which had good energy density.

N-doping significantly affects the catalytic activity of graphene. The charge distribution in N-G plays an important role in catalysis. Recent advances suggest that N-G catalytic support can improve the intrinsic activity of transition metal nanoparticles (NPs) acting as catalyst

[106]. Nitrogen was incorporated into graphitic network in different configurations by using pyridinic (Np), quaternary (Nq), pyrrolic (NPY), nitrile (NN), and vacancy pyridinic nitrogen (VNP) atom. This N-doping modified the electronic structure of transition metal NPs due to neighboring 2p-orbitals of nitrogen, which led to high absorption energy of the substances. Variation in adsorption mode of formaldehyde over Pt catalyst with N-doped carbon nanotube support is shown in Fig. 7 .

Magnetic N-G (MNG) was evaluated as a potential catalyst in the reduction of p-nitrophenol [63]. The catalytic activity of synthesized hybrid was found to increase significantly due to the loading of Fe3O4 NPs.Nucleation and growth of Fe3O4 NPs on the graphene surface generate efficient assembly between NPs and graphene. This geometric confinement of Fe3O4 NPs in graphene layers augments their interface contact and suppresses the dissolution and agglomeration of NPs, which promotes the catalytic activity and stability of MNG. It was determined that the reaction did not follow pseudo-first-order kinetics in later stages of reduction. This may be the cause of enhanced catalytic activity of MNG.

Three dimensional nitrogen doped graphene foam (3D-NGF) was employed as a catalyst in the transformation of p-nitrophenol to p-aminophenol [107]. The apparent activation energy was calculated and a reaction mechanism was proposed for hydrogenation reduction of p-nitrophenol. 3D-NGF demonstrated a high catalytic activity and a high stability. This high activity could be attributed to the synergistic effect between electronic properties induced bynitrogen-doping and3D foam-like structure.

N-doped carbon networks can also be used as catalysts to carry out energy conversion due to their low cost, better stability, and good electron-transfer capacity [108–110].Examples include fuel cells, which have attracted global interest due to their high efficiency of energy conversion. The importance of electrochemical activity in fuel cells is the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), which could be influenced by cathode catalysts. Owing to high efficiency, platinum- and platinum-based materials are used as catalysts in the fuel cells. However, less stability and high expenditure along with poor durability prohibited their commercialization. In the last few years, efforts have been made to produce Pt-free catalysts which are based on heteroatoms-doped (N, B, S)or economical metal carbon materials. Ametal-free electrocatalyst of N-Gdisplayed efficient performance towards ORR [111].

N-G for ORR as non-metalelectro-catalyst was developed by giving thermal treatment to graphene with cyanamide [112]. Effect of temperature on ORR activity of synthesized products was studied. A mechanism of ORR was explored by applying a voltammetric technique.An evaluation of electrocatalytic activity in the presence of D-gluconic acid and glucose in neutral pH medium was studied. Tolerance of N-G towards D-gluconic acid poisoning as well as glucose oxidation was ascertained. Sample, which was prepared at 1000 ° C, exhibited better electrocatalytic ORR performance compared to simple graphene networks.This increased the long term operation stability of the N-G. D-gluconic acid and glucose had better tolerance than commercial Pt/Cat neutral pH conditions. Significantly, N-G provided the attractive potential for replacing high priced Pt-based catalysts used in ORR in order to increase the industrialization possibility of fuel cells.

Nitrogen-doped three dimensional reduced graphene oxide (N-3DrGO) catalysts were prepared in which N-content was varied [113].The high content of N in N-3DrGO did not result in increased electro-catalytic activity. The N-3DrGO catalyst having mass ratio of 1:1.5 between GO and melamine showed excellent activity towards ORR due to the highest percentage of graphitic N and pyridinic N(69.2%).This resulted in solvent dispersibility along with smaller interfacial and charge transfer resistance. The obtained N-3DrGO catalyst demonstrated good catalytic performance, excellent methanol tolerance, and increased durability as compared to that of commercial Pt/C catalyst.

Microwave plasma torch, coupled with plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) method, was used to manipulate thickness and defect density of few layered graphene sheets at ~ 300 ° C[66]. Nitrogen content in N-G sheets varied from 4.7–13.3 at.%. Medium quality N-G exhibited the highest electro-ctalytic activity towards ORR; an electron transfer number obtained was 3.94, which was comparable to that of platinum. Nitrogen plasma-doped graphene showed high activity and selectivity for detecting ascorbic acid, uric acid, and dopamine.

The development of electro-catalytic materials for splitting water is an important step for generating hydrogen[114]. The hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) involved the use of catalysts such as Pt- or Pt-based materials [115].Non-noble metal catalysts have been examined to carry out HER. Examples are Mo/Co containing compounds, alloys, phosphides, and sulfides.Impregnation of these catalysts into carbon networks for obtaining carbon-based composites have been reported as an efficient strategy to increase their HER activities.

Synthesis of NiFe@N-G microtube (NiFe-NGMT) composites was carried out by metal organic framework route, and subsequently evaluated for conducting HER [53]. The synthesized NiFe-NGMT composites showed a remarkable catalytic activity with a low over potential of 70.5 mV along with small Tafel slope (63.4 mV per decade). These are important parameters for water splitting to generate hydrogen. Interesting studies were conducted to enhance catalytic activity by combining N-doped graphene carbon frameworks with different transition metals viz. Fe [116], Co [117], Ni, Cu, Pd [118] and Pt [119–121] and with various metal oxides such as MnO2 [122], Fe3O4 [123] and TiSi2Ox [124]. N-G was reported as a valuable catalyst for performing ORR [124]. Ni was used as a catalyst in N-G composite, synthesized by the CVD approach [125]. A ratio of nitrogen and carbon was found to be 4% for synthesizing synthesized N-G.This material was demonstrated as a superb electrode in the ORR associated with alkaline fuel cells. AS shown in Fig. 8 , an electrode of pristine graphene possessed two electron systems for reducing oxygen at potentials of about −0.45 and − 0.7 V. AN-graphene electrode involved a single step of four electrons and was more efficient for performing ORR [126]. The current density of N-G was found to be 3 times higher than that of commercial Pt/C electrode over a wide potential range. Also, N-G as a catalyst possessed high stability, better selectivity, and good tolerance to crossover effect. This superior performance of N-G materials could demonstrate many catalytic applications beyond fuel cells.

Besides the applications discussed in this section of N-G materials, another upcoming field is their role as microwave absorbers. N-G nanosheets anchored with Fe3O4 nanocrystals are reported as microwave absorbers [59]. Due to synergistic interaction between Fe3O4 nanocrystals and N-G with dielectric loss, the Fe3O4/N-G hybrids demonstrated improved microwave absorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Intrinsic properties of GQDs and N-GQDs can be modified for their potential uses in electronic and optical devices. Doping of GQDs with nitrogen can modify their electronic and chemical features [127]. Because of amino group-functionalization or N-doping, optical and electrical properties of GQDs change substantially.Examples include improvement of catalytic activities and appearance of up-conversion photoluminescence. Several strategies have emerged to improve photocatalytic performance of semiconductor photocatalyst such as structure design [128], noble metal loading [129], and formation of a semiconductor composite [130].Reviews on the applications of graphene and its related nanomaterials for treating various organic pollutants are available[131, 132].

Various approaches are reported for synthesizing GQDs viz hydrothermal method, acidic oxidation, oxygen plasma etching, electrochemical approach, microwave synthesis, catalyzed cage-opening, exfoliation and pyrolysis methods [133]. Among these, size of carbonaceous materials (e.g., graphite, graphite oxides, carbon nanotubes, and pith fibers) was decreased to 10 nm by a chemical oxidation procedure.A cutting process is the most widely known and the easiest method to obtain crystalline GQDs. The structures are made of graphitic carbon structure with the variety of O-containing functional groups. Quantum confinement and edge effects of these materials assist in modulating their structural, magnetic, electronic, electrochemical and optical properties [134].

The available methods for preparing GQDs are broadly classified as top-down and bottom-up strategies (Fig. 9 )[135]. Abottom-upapproach depends on the selection of individual stocks. Atop-down strategy involves the cutting of big carbonaceous material into small parts. Abottom-up approach is preferred because the size of the QDs can be controlled by this method.Many synthesis methods have been applied to synthesize GQDs.These methods are discussed in the following subsection.

GO can be used as a precursor material to prepare GQDs [136].An ultrasonication method under strong acidic conditions could oxidize graphene sheets [137]. GQDs obtained after multiple purifications showed a bright blue photoluminiscence and a diameter of 3–5 nm. Ultrasonication of GO was carried out in dimethylformamide to produce GQDs.

A solvothermal method has been applied to synthesize a variety of materials such as ceramics, metals, semiconductors, and polymers. In this process, solvent used was under moderate to high temperature and pressure, which allowed interactions among precursors. A hydrothermal method is based on the ability of water to dilute silicates, oxides, and sulfides. Using a hydrothermal process, formation of GQDs can be achieved by oxidizing graphene sheets at 200° C in order to introduce oxygen containing functional groups (e.g.,carbonyl, ketone, ether, and hydroxyl groups). Change in size of graphene sheets was observed during oxidation and reduction processes, which led to the formation of GQDs with diameter 9.6 nm.

N-GQDs were synthesized from poly-acrylonitrile-based carbon fibers (CFs) by applying a cutting method [141]. Optical properties of GQDs could be tuned by varying the heat treatment of CFs. N-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite were designed to remove methylene blue from water. A hydrothermal method used ethylene diamine and citric acid that synthesized N-GQDs. AN-GQDs/TiO2material was prepared by stirring N-GQDs with TiO2 NPs for 24 h [142].

Interaction of the electrical dipole moment with microwave energy results in the molecular rotation of polar solvent molecules, which releases heat that is used for preparing GQDs. GQDs were synthesized from GO by a microwave method under acidic conditions of HNO3 and H2SO4 [143]. A decrease in reaction time was observed using this method in comparison to that of the solvothermal process. The obtained GQDs showed a green fluorescence.However, a reduction with sodium borohydride gave blue photoluminescence of increased intensity. Amicrowave assisted hydrothermal method was employed for preparing GQDs using graphene as a precursor [144]. GQDs were processed to fabricate white light emitting diode using doped 4,4-bis(carbazol-9-yl)biphenyl as an emissive layer. Quantum efficiency of this diode was found to be 0.2%, which again suggests it as a novel phosphor to prepare light emitting diodes of solid state lighting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acid and solvent free synthesis methods
ce:section.ce:para: A use of a strong acid in reactions is a major drawback in many methods used to prepare QDs [139].It is preferable to use acid free methods for synthesizing GQDs. A method for preparing GQDs involved exfoliating graphite intercalation compounds (GICs) [145]. Potassium sodium tartrate was used as a reagent that intercalated into graphite to produce GICs with less damage to structure. After the GICs were exfoliated in water, GQDs could be produced at yield of more than 60%. GQDs could also be obtained by mixing GO with oxone, followed by sonoand photo-oxidations [146]. Using ozone, GQDs were obtained directly from graphite, carbon fibers, carbon nanotubes, and coal in acid-free conditions. A thermal reaction method was also reported to produce GQDs [147].A solvothermal redox reaction was found to convert all the carbon-based materials into GQDs. An initial yield of GQDs was ~ 10%, which decreased to 6% after second run.

A simple solvent free method has also been used to prepare N-GQDs.Free and solidified N,S-GQDs were synthesized by one step solvent free synthesis using citric acid as a precursor and L-cysteine as a dopant [148]. Graphene was first prepared by intermolecularly dehydrating citric acid. Nitrogen and sulfur were introduced into graphene network by its interaction with L-cysteine. Cross-linking, present in graphene nucleus, was due to intermolecular condensation that resulted in the formation of free N,S- GQDs.

In an electrothermal method to prepare GQDs, C-C bonds were cleaved by hydroxyl and oxygen radicals [149]. Cyclic voltammetry was employed for generating GQDs in which reduced GO was utilized as a working electrode in phosphate buffer solution. Photo-Fenton reaction was also reported for synthesizing GQDs [150]. In this method, GQDs were obtained from GOs by cutting GOs using hydroxyl radicals.Most of the oxygen containing groups were removed from GOupon the completion of the reaction and the resulting GQDs showed blue photoluminescence.

An electron beam irradiation involves beta radiation of high energy to synthesize materials (Fig. 11 ). A room temperature electron beam irradiation strategy for preparing single crystalline fluorescent GQDs involved 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and hydrazine hydrate as starting materials [151]. Synthesized GQDs exhibited fluorescence at 475 nm with quantum yield of 32%. Quantum yields of GQDs were dependent on conditions of preparation such as dose of irradiation, quality of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, and concentration of hydrazine. A simple and cost effective thermal pyrolysis technique was used for synthesizing N-GQDs using a lemon juice [152]. N-GQDs showed excitation dependent conversion fluorescence. A strong fluorescence emission was displayed by N-GQDs at a wavelength of 340 nm. By the addition of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol, a highly selective and a sensitive quenching was observed in the fluorescence spectrum of N-GQDs.

Apart of the methods discussed in this section, another upcoming approach is the utilization of plant based methods. Carbon dots have been prepared from Celery leaf extract using hydrothermal treatment. Carbon dots prepared at lower temperature displayed smaller particle size distribution with less photoluminescence emission intensity as compared to their counterparts at higher temperature [153]. In another report, bee-pollen, citric acid and glucose were used as precursors for the synthesis of carbon QDs [154].Aloe was used as a carbon source in another study for preparing water soluble and high fluorescent carbon QDs that had spherical shape with an average diameter of 5 nm [155]. A simple, economical, and green synthesis of QDs was carried out from waste of pomelo peel using a hydrothermal method [156].

An overview of methods reveals that the synthesis of GQDs can be accomplished mainly by applying top down strategy (e.g.,solvothermal, hydrothermal, electron beam irradiation, exfoliation of graphene and microwave methods).A few reports on the bottom up synthesis of GQDs have also been employed, which use hydrothermal and solvothermal synthesis from starting materials. A bottom up approach is considered better than top down approach as the former resulted in less defects on the surface of graphene that could provide numerous applications.However, these methods are less economical in comparison with top down approaches [157].

Just like N-G, the properties are divided into two categories viz. conductive and optical properties. Conductive properties determine their applications in batteries and supercapacitors. Because GQDs possess optical properties, they are used in sensors, photocatalytical processes, and solar cells. GQDs also have photoluminescence due to quantum confinement of the electrons and are used in tracers and imaging. The properties are briefly discussed in the following subsections.

GQDs can be used in electrochemical capacitors because of their remarkable properties like good electrical conductivity, high surface area, mobility, tunable band gap and good dispersibility in different solvents [158–159]. There are generally two types of supercapacitors based on charge storage mechanism i.e. double layer capacitors and pseudocapacitors. The GQDs-based supercapacitor was derived from doped-polyaniline composites (GQDP) [160]. These prepared GQDP composites are nanotubular or nanofibrous and exhibited high specific capacitance (80.1%) after 3000 cycles.

Asymmetric GQD/MnO2 capacitor showed two times higher specific capacitance than symmetric GQD capacitor (110.74 μFcm−2) with the energy density of 0.154 μWhcm−2 [161]. GQD-HNT (GQDs-halloysite nanotubes) nanocomposite was fabricated in order to provide increased charge storage sites as well as to allow fast charge transport for its high-performance supercapacitor applications [162]. Surface morphology of composite showed that the GQDs were homogeneously distributed and condensed on the surface of HNTs via amide linkage. This composite provided better electroactive sites, lower resistance capacitance, and good electron density. Electrochemical behavior of this nanocomposite was studied employing cyclic voltammetry (CV). Fig. 12a shows the CV curves of GQD-HNT composite electrode in electrolyte solution of 1 M Na2SO4 at different rates ranging from 5 to 100 mV/s. These curves possess rectangular shapes; indicating electrical double layer capacitor and depicting the greater rate capability and good electrochemical reversible behavior of GQD-HNT nanocomposite. Fig. 12b demonstrates that the GQD-HNT composite behaves as a good capacitor with specific capacitance of 323 F/g at the rate of 5 mV/s.Specific capacitance decreased to 186 F/g on increase in scan rate to 100 mV/s, which corresponds to the retention of 58%.An enhancement in specific conductance of GQD-HNTs was mainly due to the presence of conducting GQDs.In another study, GQD film on glassy carbon electrode was prepared and specific capacitance of 108 F/g was obtained. Specific capacitance of the GQDD-HNTs was found to be much greater than the simple GQD.Results clearly showedthe superiority of GQD-HNTs over GQDs as elecroactive nanomaterials in supercapacitors.

Galvanometric charge-discharge measurements were performed on nanocomposite in 1 M Na2SO4 solution at different current densities ranging from 0.5 to 20 A/g (Fig. 12c). These curves indicate the symmetric nature and high capacitance of GQD-HNT; suggesting low IR drop of this nanocomposite. A specific capacitance of GQD-HNT as a function of applied current density is shown in Fig. 12d. This nanomaterial exhibited the capacitances of 363, 338, 294, 272, 258, 246, 233 and 216 F/g at 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 and 20 A/g. A 60% capacitance of GQD-HNT nanocomposite was retained at a current density of 20 A/g. This indicates that nanocomposite was stable over a wide range of current densities. No IR drop was seen, which suggests that the GQD-HNTs possessed a small internal resistance and an efficient extraction of stored energy.

GQD anchored VO2 array electrode (GVG) has been utilized in the formation of Li ion battery [163, 164] (Fig. 13 a1). Negatively charged GQDs with carboxyl groups on surface were immobilized on the positively charged surface of VO2 array via an electrophoresis deposition. A deposition of VO2 resulted in high electric conductivity.An effect of GQDs was found to be similar to that of supercapacitors that separated the VO2 nanobelts from each other and avoided agglomeration, thereby minimized the dissolution (Fig. 13 a2). A Coulombic efficiency of Li ion battery was 99% initially and then 100% in subsequent cycles with a specific capacity of 389 mAhg−1.With increasing current density from 1/3C to 120C, 36% capacitance was retained. Moreover, after 1500 cycles at 18 Ag−1, retention of GVG electrode was 94% (Fig. 13 a3). A Na+ion battery, based on this GVG electrode, exhibited capacity of 306 mAhg−1 with the retention of 88% after 1500 cycles.

Composites of CuO/Cu and CuO/Cu/GQDs nanowire were synthesized to study their applications in Li ion batteries [165]. The conductive properties of the composites led to decrease in electrochemical impedance and facilitation in transport kinetics.Compared to structure of CuO/Cu composite, a nanowire electrode showed the coulomb efficiency of 87%. Copper foam that acted as a current collector provided a better conductivity of electrons.

Ionic liquid-capped GQDs (IL-GQDs) and their use as a fluorescent probe has been reported for the detection of Fe(CN)6 3− anion [166].In this study,1-butyl-3-methyl imidazolium tetrafluoroborate (BMIMBF4) was chosen as a model ionic liquid.Photoluminescence behavior of GQDs showed no alternation on the combination with ionic liquid. However, a detection of ions using IL-GQDs increased that allowed the detection of Fe(CN)6 3− using IL-GQDs. A wide range of sensitivity from 1.0 × 10−7 to 2.5 × 10−3 mol l−1 with a detection limit of 40 nmol l−1 was observed.

Fe3O4/GQDs nanocomposite was prepared in order to sense amino acids [167]. Synthesized GQDs and Fe3O4-GQDs were electrodeposited on a carbon electrode by cyclic voltammetry in potential range of −1.0 to 1.0 V. Fe3O4-GQDs nanocomposite provided an enhancement in electrochemical signals of amino acids due to a synergistic effect between GQDs and Fe3O4. It was observed that the Fe3O4-GQDs showed much higher electroactivity and the detection of amino acid was about 10 folds higher than normal the GQDs.

Solar cell is a photovoltaic device, which is dependent on the efficiency of photogenerated carriers. GQDs absorb visible light and hence can be used in solar cells [168]. Many types of solar cells have been reported (e.g.,organic solar cell [169, 170], silicon based solar cell [171], dye sensitized solar cell [172, 173] and many more [168, 174, 175].

Silicon-based solar cells are gaining interest due to their high conversion efficiency. A GQDs/silicon nanowire array core-shell device was prepared that had high conversion efficiency of 9.1%, similar to silicon-based solar cells [176].The photo conversion efficiency (PCE) of 6.63% was obtained (Fig. 14a).The improvement in performance of GQDs compared to GO hybrids and bare silicon could be elucidated by understanding the energy band structure of GQDs (Fig. 14b). Size of GQDs plays very important role when the open circuit voltage (Voc) of the device increases.A short circuit current (Jsc) decreases with a decrease in size of GQDs. This effect indicates that hetero-junction barrier increases with a decrease in size and the barrier of hole transformation increases, which results in increase in VOC and decrease in JSC, respectively. The high performance of GQDs in comparison with bare Si and GO was due to energy band structure possessed by GQDs, which behaved as an electron blocking layer [177].A size of GQDs also played important role as the voltage of the device increased with decrease in size while a decrease in the current of short circuit was observed.

GQDs may be alternate to silicon-, perovskite-, and TiO2-based dye sensitized solar cells due to their high chemical stability [178], low cost [179], low toxicity, and biocompatibility [180].A size dependent band gap tuning, quantum confinement, and edge effect are the main factors for their uses in solar cells. Many solar cells based on GQDs have been synthesized such as silicon/GQDs hetero junction solar cell [181], polymer doped GQDs solar cell, and semiconductor/GQDs solar cell [182]. GQDs are also involved in sensitizers, active layer additive in acceptor/donor blends [183], and electron/hole transport agents [184].

Combinations of organic/inorganic solar cells with metal oxide and organic semiconductors have achieved great importance. But it remains a challenge to match the energy levels of donor-acceptor system. The incorporation of GQDs into organic/inorganic layers increased the conversion of power efficiency [170, 175]. Fig. 15a depicts the alignment of different energy levels that facilitated the separation of photogenerated carriers [173]. Oxides and organics can partially show solar energy absorption but insertion of GQDs results in broader absorption spectra to increase in harvesting incident light irradiation. These factors play crucial role in the performance of solar cells (see Fig. 15b).

Different TiO2 nanostructures decorated on carbon dots and GQDs have been synthesized [185–187] to enhance photocatalytic water splitting into H2 and O2.For example, water splitting by CDs/C3N4 composite under visible light irradiation.A maximum quantum efficiency of 16% was obtained and the solar energy conversion efficiency was 2% [188]. Fig. 16a shows the mechanism involved in the reaction of water splitting by the CDs/C3N4 composite. Two step reactions involving two electrons led to the generation of H2. A C3N4 material was responsible for the first step and CDs were responsible for the second step. An increase in light absorbance was due to CDs.A synergistic effect of CDs and C3N4, i.e.,appropriate band gap, porous structure of C3N4, and high stability made these composites good photocatalysts for visible-light driven water splitting. An increase in quantum efficiency (QE) was consistent with the absorbance spectrum (Fig. 16b). A value of QE of the catalyst containing 4.8 × 10−3 g CDs/g-catalyst increased by 20% relative to QE value for catalyst with QDs concentration of1.6 × 10−5 g CDs/g-catalyst.

GQDs have been examined as energy delay antennas to obtain high power conversion efficiency of Ru based N719 acceptor dye. Light harvesting efficiency was increased by the preparation of donor materials in Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based dye sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) [189].The efficiency of FRET was observed to be 27%. GQDs co-sensitizedDSSC achieved an efficiency of 7.96% with Jsc of 16.54 mAcm−2, which was 30% higher than that of a N719-based DSSC. A charge recombination was reduced by GQDs that caused increase in open-circuit voltage upto 770 mV. It was observed that the increased absorption of GQDs antennas led to improved short circuit current density (Jsc) in the UV–Vis region.RET/FRET and the synergistic effect contributed to appreciable increase of Jsc in the UV region.

GQDs showed excitation independent photoluminescent behavior under the wavelength range of 320–420 nm. A photocatalytic activity of prepared GQDs was determined on the basis of degradation of methylene blue (MB). The photocatalytic rates of GQDs, GQDs-PEGs, and GQDs-PEIs were found to decrease successively. Polymer modified GQDs quantitatively controlled the degradation of MB [190].Hydroxyl-GQDs (GQDs) modified mesoporous graphitic carbon nitride (mpg-C3N4) composites were also prepared [191].A composite of 0.5 wt% GQDs/mpg-C3N4 showed higher photocatalytic activity to degrade tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and rhodamine under visible light irradiation than pure mpg-C3N4. Enhanced photocatalytic activity was because of uniform dispersion of GQDs on the surface of mpg-C3N4 and the intimate contact between the two materials. Electron spin resonance measurements andradical trapping experiments demonstrated that the GQDs/mpg-C3N4 composites couldgenerate more O2 ●- species and holes to cause degradation of target pollutants efficiently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs have abundant edges, good crystal structure, and large surface area,whichprovide good conductivity and edge active sites. Nitrogen doping in GQDs createdopportunities to advance electrochemical and optoelectronic applications [192].Different methods used for GQDs and N-GQDs and their applications are summarized in Table2 . The applications of N-GQDs as sensors, electrodes and photocatalysts are one of the main research areas, which are discussed in these subsections.

Sensors are the materials used to detect the changes occurring in the environment. N-GQDs have shown potential application in sensor and optoelectronic field because of their stable electrocatalytic and electrochemiluminescence activities. A new electrochemiluminescence signal-on method, based on N-GQDs and chitosan,has been reported for sensing nitroaniline [193]. Chitosan exhibited high hydrophilic property, good adhesion, and high water permeability for loading N-GQDs over surface of glassy carbon electrode. N-GQDs were highly active catalyst in fast diazotization reactions that involved anilines. Diazotization reaction of nitroanilineresulted in electrochemiluminescence enhancement of N-GQDs/chitosan-modified electrode.

Solid N,S-GQDs showed favorable photoluminescence behavior, which was stable over a broad range of pH.High tolerance of solid N,S-GQDs towards external ionic strength upto 1.0 mol l−1 NaClmade it a good fluorescence probe for detecting Fe3+ ions.The solid N,S-GQDs also showedfluorescence with 10.6% quantum yield. Carbonization assisted green N-GQDs were used to develop fluorescent probes for sensing 2,4,6- trinitrophenol (TNP) in aqueous medium [151, 194].Linearity for TNP was in the range of 0–16 μM with detection limit of 0.92 μM.

N-GQDs in the aqueous solutiongave intense blue emission and excitation independent photoluminescence behaviors [195]. A fluorescence quantum yield of N-GQDs was found to be upto 75.2%, which was much higher than that reported for GQDs materials.N-GQDs- modified glassy carbon electrode (N-GQD/CD) was used fordetection of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) [196]. An electrochemical reduction of TNT by N-GQDs/CD was affected by different parameters such as solution and accumulation time. An accumulation time for detecting TNT on N-GQDs/CD electrode increased in steps until a maximum value of electrochemical reduction current was obtained.A current response for reducing TNT was found to be a maximum at an accumulation time of 150 s(Fig. 17a).Therefore, this was chosen as an optimum time. In the pH range of 5.0 to 9.0, current density increased up to pH 7.0 after which it decreased up to pH 9.0 An optimal response was found at pH 7.0(Fig. 17b). A buffer solution at pH 7.0 was selected to detect TNT. A linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) response of N-GQDs/CD electrode at optimized accumulation conditions with increasing TNT concentration is shown in Fig. 17c. A reduction current increased with an increase in concentration of TNT.A cathodic peak of −0.36 V, shown in Fig. 17d, was used to assay signal, which responded linearly to TNT at a lower concentration and became saturated at higher concentration. Aresponse displayed a linear range from 1 to 400 μg l−1 (r2 = 0.999). Adetection limit was found to be 0.2 μg l−1at a signal to noise ratio of 3,0, which was very less to detect TNT.Basically, a detection limit of N-GQDs-basedTNT sensors was far better than that of simple TNT sensors.

A relative standard deviation (RSD) of the GQDs/CD electrode has also been reported from the calibration plots obtained using five freshly prepared electrodes.Results revealed the reproducibility in the electrode. The RSD observed was ~ 0.5%.This electrode was also examined for its stability.In tests, the electrode was kept in a buffer at 4° C when it was not in use for 1–2 days and its response was recorded at 100 μg l−1 TNT. Response was found to decrease in first few days (3 days) and after that response remained constant and the electrode retained 92% of its response even after storage of a month. This means that the electrode can be used as a stable sensor for detecting TNT.

Doped GQDs have electro-catalytic activity towards oxidation-reduction reactions (ORR). Examples include N-[197–202], B-[203], B,N-co-doped [204–210] and S-doped [211, 212] graphene-basedmaterials.Fig. 18 shows the polarization curves for reducing oxygen onto pure (a)GQDs and (b)B-GQDs, (c)N-GQDs, and (d)B,N-GQDs [213].Oxygen was present in saturated 0.1 M KOH solution. The system showed well defined cathodic features for irreversible reducing oxygen. Doped-GQDs displayed a positive shift of onset potential compared topure GQDs, indicating that the doping with heteroatoms increased the catalytic activity of ORR. Boron-doped GQDs attract nucleophilic oxygen molecules on the surface of GQDs, leading to chemisorption.
In the graphitic π-network, shuttling of electron occurs at boron sites through the pz orbital of boron to chemisorbed O2 molecule.

Nitrogen-based defects in graphene increase the π state density near Fermi level, which reduces the work function. A high electron-withdrawing capacity of N atom reduces electron density of adjacent C nuclei and charge transfer takes place from C to N.Nitrogen atom can back donate the electrons to Cpz orbitals, which are involved in π-conjugated system. This process facilitates the adsorption of oxygen on C atom next to N atom, which leads to dissociation of oxygen. Due to loss of electro-neutrality of graphitic network, favorable sites for adsorption of oxygen are created to enhance activity of ORR by N-GQDs.Significantly, boron-nitrogen-doped GQDs; possess the highest activity for performing ORR in comparison to other materials because of the synergistic effect of boron and nitrogen. Photoemission data indicated that the presence of nitrogen determines the shift towards higher binding energy in the centroid of photoemission peak of boron. Furthermore, the presence of positive charges on boron sites could cause moreadsorption of oxygen.An increase in the pyridinic component of N-GQDs apparently stabilized by the presence of boron, suggesting the presence of sites are suitable for performing ORR.

N-GQDs/graphene hybrid was prepared hydrothermally for immobilizing N-GQDs on graphene.A3D conductive network was constructed [214]. N-GQDs-graphene showed a high electro-catalytic performance and long term stability. N-GQDs hybrid played a good role in ORR, especially that made by using pyridine as a nitrogen precursor. N-GQDs have their uses in electro-catalytic activity [215]. B- and N-doped GQDs possessed high electrical conductivity and high surface area, which made the resulting material an oxygen reduction electro-catalysts [216]. One step synthesis of N-GQDs was reported using a solvothermal cutting method from poly-acrylonitrile (PAN)-based CFs[141]. The synthesized N-GQDs were fabricated with PEDOT:PSS (poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate) which acted as anodic buffer layer and induced hole and energy-down-drift. This provided the enhance deficiency from 7.5% to 8.5%. N-GQDs are considered as excellent probes for high performance optoelectronic applications.

Batteries are electrochemical energy sources and are used in various electronic devices. A performance of the battery depends on the electrode material, thus the development of new electrode material with increased performance has become the concern of battery industry [217]. Li, Ti, N and Sco-doped graphene quantum dots (LTO/N,S-GQDs) were shown as an anodic material for Li ion batteries [218]. Doping of GQDs showed synergistic effect that could increase the electron transfer by a stronger coupling of electron between S and Nco-doped GQDs and LTO anode.An excellent electrochemical performance was exhibited by this anode material in Li ion batteries. Discharge capacity of LTO/N,S-GQDs electrode was observed to be 254.2 mAhg−1 at 0.1C. This value was found to be higher than that of LTO/GQDs electrode (225.7 mAhg−1) and LTO-C electrode (169 mAhg−1). This indicates that the incorporation of N,S-GQDsgreatly increased the specific capacity of electrode. On comparing N,S-GQDs with graphene, it was observed that higher specific surface area and more C atoms in N,S-GQDs resulted in its higher storagecapacity. Charge-discharge curves of LTO-C electrode and LTO/GQDs electrodes were studied at different rates ranging from 0.1C to 10C. At 0.1C, discharge capacity was more compared to charge capacity, whereas at other rates, discharge capacity was almost equal to charge capacity and a good coulombic efficiency was observed (Fig. 19 ). A flat potential plateau was observed at the rate of 0.1 and 0.5C. This plateau was short and it bend down with increase in rate while the plateau of LTO/N,S-GQDs electrode remained constant. This was because of polarization observed in LTO-C electrode that increased with increase in rate. It was predicted from these results that the GQDs can be used for various electrochemical applications.

N-GQDs can also be used as photocatalysts for splitting water under visible light irradiation [219]. N-GQDs possess bandgap of 2.2 eV; favorable to absorption of visible light. Presence of nitrogen atoms in graphene network resulted in n-type conductivity. Groups on surface produced p-type conductivity Fig. 20a. It means p-n junction is realized in the confined regions and sp2 clusters plays the role of junction with ohmic contact. The n- and p-domains are usually responsible to produce H2 and O2 Fig. 20b,c. This can be further confirmed by results obtained from a separate evolution of H2 and O2. Such metal free catalyst can provide environmentally friendly technique for splitting water under visible light irradiation.

S, N-GQDs, linked with graphitic carbon (g-C3N4) exhibited higher photocatalytic activity in the degradation of phodamine B dye under visible light irradiation that degradation using g-C3N4under same conditions [220]. Increase in photodegradation was due to the proper separation of photogenerated holes and electrons in g-C3N4/S, N-GQD composites.

N-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite was used as a catalyst for photodegradation of methylene blue. N-GQDs showed an excellent effect on the photocatalytic behavior of TiO2. Due to addition of N-GQDs to TiO2 NPs, TiO2 showed increased photodegradation efficiency from 40 to 85% towards methylene blue under UV light irradiation. The N-GQDs-BiVO4/g-C3N4-Z scheme heterojunction was prepared for photocatalytic degrading antibiotics (e.g.,tetracycline,oxytetracycline, and ciprofloxacin.This heterojunction showed the efficiency of 91.5% degradation of tetracycline in 30min [221].

GQDs are made up of single or very less graphene lattices, which include quantum confinement effect and edge effects. Comparatively, graphene is a 2D monolayer of carbon network. GQDs show photoluminescence, which was not observed in graphene. Photoluminescence requires that the electrons abruptly drop the significant amount of energy. Graphene is a semi-metal having zero band gap due to delocalization of electrons. Lots of states in graphene are available to scatter, which prevents it from exhibiting photoluminescence. Quantum dots on the other hand have space constrained in all directions and have high energy separation due to which they exhibit photoluminescence behavior.

A doping of heteroatom to graphene and GQDs results in advantageous properties of the carbon-based nanomaterials. This doping strategy showed enhanced applications in energy and environmental fields. Many synthetic strategies for N-G and GQDs were reported which tailor their structures and properties. Future development in the field of synthesis of N-GQDs and N-G should be aimed at greener approaches. This would lead to elimination of residual reagents such as acids.

N-G and N-GQDs could be synergistically integrated with metals, metal oxides, metal sulfide, metal nitrides, semiconductors, and polymers to produce functional materials.These materials showed superior prospects in various applications such as sensors, batteries, fuel cells, solar cells, and photocatalytical processes. A significant progress has been made in demonstrating applications of N-G and N-GQDs, but in-depth studies on the mechanism of interaction of nitrogen with graphene and its subsequent effect on the properties of graphenes are still elusive.Future studies may include investigations to learn many physicochemical properties of N-G and GQDs. Studies supported with theoretical interpretations may facilitate future work on controllable doping of graphene and GQDs for their efficient use as energy source and as materials in environmental applications.

This review on GQDs and N-GQDs revealed that their potential as energy devices and photocatalysts has been studied and a future research on co-doping with other heteroatoms may be performed.Additionally, research on properties of the nitrogen-doped graphene and GQDs have been mainly on conductive properties, but exploiting their optical properties in photocatalytical processes is scarce. More efforts may be made on taking advantage of nitrogen-doped carbon materials in the field of photocatalysis to remediate pollutants in water. Furthermore, N-G and N-GQDs have rarely been explored in inactivating the microorganisms in air and water. Environmental studies may be extended to seek the role of N-G and N-GQDs in disinfecting air and water.
